meta.source: Wine Folly — Wine Descriptions & What They Mean
meta.graph_integration: KNOWLEDGE-05 — entity-centric ontology; descriptor graph is one wine-domain subgraph

categories.name: Inorganic
categories.introduction: Non-fruit, mineral, and textural descriptors — the stoniness, petrol, and tactile qualities that define terroir and winemaking texture.

categories.name: Flower
categories.introduction: Floral aromatics from grape and fermentation — violet in Cabernet, citrus blossom in Riesling, and perfumed whites.

categories.name: Herb
categories.introduction: Herbaceous, green, and savory vegetal notes — from grassy Sauvignon Blanc to bell pepper pyrazines in Carmenère.

categories.name: Oak
categories.introduction: Barrel-derived flavors and textures — toast, vanilla, spice, and creamy malolactic notes from oak aging.

categories.name: Fruit
categories.introduction: Primary fruit character organized by ripeness, color family, and fruit type — the aromatic core of most wine descriptions.

categories.name: Spice
categories.introduction: Spice and aromatic warmth — pepper, clove, cinnamon, and exotic notes from grape, oak, or fermentation.

categories.name: Alcohol
categories.introduction: Alcohol perception — heat, ripeness, and viscosity cues including legs and jammy over-ripeness.

categories.name: Acidity
categories.introduction: Acid structure on a continuum from flat to nervy — the backbone that drives food pairing more than any other single trait.

categories.name: Tannin
categories.introduction: Phenolic grip and texture — from silky Pinot to aggressive young Nebbiolo; tannin is the main red-wine pairing lever.

categories.name: Style
categories.introduction: Overall personality — earthy vs. polished, rustic vs. refined, accessible vs. closed.

categories.name: Yeast
categories.introduction: Fermentation and lees character — biscuit in Champagne, creamy MLF whites, and sourdough-like autolytic notes.

categories.name: Body
categories.introduction: Perceived weight and density — the mouthfeel scale from thin to fat that must track dish heaviness.
